As fundamental and essential matrices figure 4. FSNet architecture has four components. The scale-invariant metric of translation error is the angular distance between the estimated and the ground truth translation vectors.
FSNet architecture has four components. A CNN feature extractor computes the feature maps f A and f B from input images A and B at 1/4 of input resolution.
The extracted features are then processed by the transformer block, which contains Nt self and cross-attention layers.
The epipolar cross-attention layer applies cross-attention along the epipolar lines and embeds Fi into the feature maps f A i.
The pose regressor applies a ResNet and a 2D average pooling block.
The vectors are combined through a max pooling operator, and the final MLP layer predicts the eRi errors associated to Fi.
There are multiple error criteria for fitting fundamental matrices. Reprojection error (RE) measures the minimum distance correction needed to align the corre-
Reprojection error (RE) measures the minimum distance correction needed to align the correspondences with the epipolar geometry, which is crucial for fundamental matrices.
Symmetric epipolar (SED) measures geometric distance of each point to its epipolar line, providing geometric information for fundamental matrices.
Sampson (RE1) distance is a first-order approximation of RE, which influences the quality of fundamental matrices.
The quality of fundamental matrices is measured with one scalar value through error criteria, affecting how they are evaluated and compared.
Choosing the best error criteria as training signal for FSNet involves considering relative pose error, one of the fundamental matrix error criteria.
Deciding on the error criteria for evaluating fundamental matrices involves comparing relative pose error and other fundamental matrix error criteria such as SED error.
Access to multiple oracle models for different error criteria in Table 1 provides a way to predict the quality of fundamental matrices accurately.
Determining which oracle model provides the best fundamental matrix scoring approach is essential for evaluating the accuracy of the fundamental matrices.
Using SP-SG and MAGSAC++ to mine fundamental matrix hypotheses from validation datasets allows for a comprehensive evaluation of the fundamental matrices.

The evaluation of mined fundamental matrices using oracle models in ScanNet and MegaDepth image pairs shows that fundamental matrix error criteria under-perform compared to relative pose error metrics.

node_2: feature extractor, transformer, epipolar cross-attention, and pose error regressor
edge: Figure 4 shows the four major components of FSNet architecture: the feature extractor, the transformer, the epipolar cross-attention, and the pose error regressor.

The feature extractor is a standard convolutional architecture as in [50], it follows the Unet-style network [45] design with skip and residual connections [28] and computes feature maps at 1/4 of the input resolution.
Before feature extraction, we center-crop and resize the input images A and B to a resolution of (H, W ) (in our experiments we use (256, 256)).

node_1: c-dimensional feature maps f a and f b
edge: Images are then processed by the convolutional feature extractor to produce the C-dimensional feature maps f A and f B.
We use an L multi-head attention transformer architecture following [50], and alternate between self and cross-attention blocks to exploit the similarities within and across.
The network graph architecture alternates between self and cross-attention blocks to exploit similarities within and across the feature maps.
The transformed features are denoted as †f A and †f B and are used in the computation by some features to generate queries (Q) and different features to compute keys (K) and values (V).

The Linear Transformer is utilized to reduce the computational complexity of the original Transformer by making use of the associativity property of matrix products and replacing the exponential similarity kernel with a linear dot-product kernel.
In the self-attention layer, the input feature map is used to compute queries (Q), keys (K), and values (V), which are then passed through a two-layer MLP for further processing.

In the cross-attention layer, the previous process of computing queries (Q), keys (K), and values (V) is repeated for a different feature map, and the result is then concatenated with the input feature map for further processing.

Time analysis. Given a pair of images and 500 hypotheses, FSNet scores and ranks them in 0.97s on a machine with one V100 GPU.
The hypotheses are queried in batches, so the epipolar cross-attention and pose error regressor subnetworks run in 167.13ms for a batch of 100 fundamental matrices.
Besides, strategies such as Candidate filter can reduce FSNet time by only scoring promising hypotheses, e.g., for fundamental estimation it only adds 150ms on top of MAGSAC++ runtime (71ms).
We introduce FSNet, a correspondence-free two-view geometry scoring method.
Our experiments show that our learned model achieves SOTA results, performing exceptionally well in challenging scenarios with few or unreliable correspondences.

Naive combinations of FSNet with traditional inlier counting lead to even higher reliability. This suggests that more sophisticated combinations of scoring approaches could lead to further improvements.
Alternatively, a better network design may improve prediction accuracy, which could supersede inlier counting in all scenarios.
The first stages of our pipeline (feature extraction, self and cross-attention blocks) form a subnetwork that precom.
Feature maps are precomputed for each image pair using the subnetwork, enabling efficient processing of the features related to the image pairs.
Local feature extraction is performed on the precomputed feature maps, allowing the extraction of specific features from the images.
Feature matching is carried out using the precomputed feature maps, facilitating the comparison and matching of features across different images.
Hypothesis generation is supported by the precomputed feature maps, aiding in the creation of hypotheses based on the extracted features.
The entire network is trained on two tasks: estimating good correspondences and two-view geometry scoring, leading to potentially enhanced performance on both tasks through synergistic improvements.
